rules.exact_text: UK Only
rules.reference: Windsor Framework: Information for pharmacists and medicines procurers (DHSC/MHRA), Labelling and packaging section

rules.exact_text: UK Only (may be applied using a sticker until 30 June 2025)
rules.reference: Windsor Framework: Information for pharmacists and medicines procurers (DHSC/MHRA), Labelling and packaging section

Windsor Framework: Information for pharmacists and medicines procurers (DHSC/MHRA), 2D barcodes and serial numbers section (EU FMD features no longer apply from 1 Jan 2025; optional non-EU-recognised 2D barcode/serial number may remain)

rules.exact_text: If an INN recommended by the World Health Organisation exists for the active moiety the English version of the name should be used exactly as published without omissions or abbreviations.
rules.reference: MHRA guidance (Chunk 2), Section 18 Use of INN or BAN

rules.exact_text: If a Modified INN (INNM) recommended by the World Health Organisation has been published for the active moiety, it should be used within the name of the medicinal product exactly as published without omissions or abbreviations.
rules.reference: MHRA guidance (Chunk 2), Section 18 Use of INN or BAN

rules.exact_text: In the UK, the usual practice is to name the product as the INN followed by the strength and the pharmaceutical form; this appears "together" with the name of the MAH by means of the information on the rest of the packaging.
rules.reference: MHRA guidance (Chunk 2), Section 19 Generic Naming Convention

rules.exact_text: The common name or scientific name should appear together with a trademark or the name of the Marketing Authorisation Holder (MAH).
rules.reference: MHRA guidance (Chunk 2), Section 19 Generic Naming Convention

rules.exact_text: Where the applicant wishes to incorporate the company name in the generic name... the company name or trademark must be positioned directly after the INN/common name.
rules.reference: MHRA guidance (Chunk 2), Section 19 Generic Naming Convention

rules.exact_text: Inclusion of a company name within the name of the medicine will not be permitted when there is not sufficient space on the packaging.
rules.reference: MHRA guidance (Chunk 2), Section 19 Generic Naming Convention

rules.exact_text: Article 56(a) of Directive 2001/83/EC, as amended, requires that the name of a medicinal product must be expressed in Braille format on the packaging.
rules.reference: Directive 2001/83/EC Article 56(a); MHRA guidance (Chunk 2), Section 20

rules.exact_text: Where a medicine is available in more than one strength, these data should also appear in Braille on the label.
rules.reference: Directive 2001/83/EC Article 56(a); MHRA guidance (Chunk 2), Section 20

rules.exact_text: Where there are concerns as to the length of the information to appear on the packaging in Braille... there is provision for use of the accepted EDQM agreed short term on small packaging.
rules.reference: MHRA guidance (Chunk 2), Section 21 "Short term" on Packaging

rules.exact_text: MHRA considers that it is acceptable to use the approved EDQM shortened terms on all packaging as long as this shortened term is included in Section 3 of the SmPC. This applies to both the text of the printed label and the Braille requirements.
rules.reference: MHRA guidance (Chunk 2), Section 21 "Short term" on Packaging

rules.exact_text: The guidance recommends the use of Marburg Medium as the Braille font of choice.
rules.reference: MHRA guidance (Chunk 2), Section 22 Marburg Medium Braille Cell Spacing

rules.exact_text: The dot diameter is 1.6mm; the dot spacing is exactly 2.5mm from dot centre to dot centre; the space between two characters is 6.0mm; the line spacing is 10.0mm; word spacing is 12.0mm.
rules.reference: MHRA guidance (Chunk 2), Section 22 Marburg Medium Braille Cell Spacing

rules.exact_text: When choosing a name for a medicine it is important to consider how this name will appear on the pack when translated into Braille and to ensure that there is sufficient space on the pack to accommodate the name chosen.
rules.reference: MHRA guidance (Chunk 2), Section 22 Marburg Medium Braille Cell Spacing

rules.exact_text: INN + MAH + strength + standard term
rules.reference: MHRA guidance (Chunk 3): Prolonged-release preparations may be named generically, including the name of the MAH e.g. INN + MAH + strength + standard term, where criteria are met

rules.exact_text: INN + the name of the MAH
rules.reference: MHRA guidance (Chunk 3): Category 1/2 AEDs should be marketed/prescribed by brand or INN + the name of the MAH

rules.exact_text: brand or INN + the name of the MAH
rules.reference: MHRA guidance (Chunk 3): Category 1/2 AEDs should be marketed/prescribed by brand or INN + the name of the MAH

rules.exact_text: switching between formulations under specialist advice
rules.reference: MHRA guidance (Chunk 3): wording such as "switching between formulations under specialist advice" may be included as a specific measure
Human Medicines Regulations 2012 (HMRs) Regulation 257 and Schedule 24; MHRA Best Practice Guidance section 4.1
HMRs Regulation 257 and Schedule 24; MHRA Best Practice Guidance section 4.1
HMRs Regulation 257 and Schedule 24; MHRA Best Practice Guidance section 4.1
MHRA Best Practice Guidance section 4.2 (Critical information co-location)
HMRs Regulation 259; MHRA Best Practice Guidance section 4.2.1 (Braille)
MHRA Best Practice Guidance section 4.2.1 (full name on at least three non-opposing faces)
MHRA Best Practice Guidance section 4.2.2 (strength expression and consistency)
MHRA Best Practice Guidance section 4.2.3 (route as registered in SmPC; positive messages; standard abbreviations)
MHRA Best Practice Guidance section 4.2.5; HMRs Schedule 24 Part 1 Paragraph 11
HMRs Schedule 24 Part 1 Paragraph 7 (Excipients of known effect must be included on the label)
MHRA Best Practice Guidance section 4.3 (critical information as large a font as possible; order; not broken up)
MHRA Best Practice Guidance section 4.3 (critical information as large a font as possible; order; not broken up)
MHRA Best Practice Guidance section 4.3 (critical information as large a font as possible; order; not broken up)
MHRA Best Practice Guidance section 4.3 (critical information as large a font as possible; order; not broken up)
HMRs Schedule 24 Part 1 Paragraph 9; MHRA Best Practice Guidance section 4.5 (space for dispensing label 35mm x 70mm usually in UK)

rules.exact_text: Only positive statements should appear on medicines labelling to avoid ambiguity of the message. For example, "For intravenous use only". Negative statements such as "Not for intravenous use" should not be used.
rules.reference: MHRA guidance (Chunk 2), section 4.6

rules.exact_text: QR codes may be included on packaging provided they are subordinate in prominence and placement to the statutory information and must link to information compliant with Regulation 261 of the HMRs (compatible with the SmPC, useful for the patient, non-promotional).
rules.reference: HMRs Regulation 261; MHRA guidance (Chunk 2), section 7.1

rules.exact_text: Inclusion of a QR code on the label or in the PIL cannot be achieved by means of a notification; an application must be made and must include in the dossier a detailed account of the information to which this code links.
rules.reference: MHRA guidance (Chunk 2), section 7.1

rules.exact_text: An email address may be included as a means of contacting the marketing authorisation holder and where a patient information leaflet is not separately available this may appear on the labelling.
rules.reference: MHRA guidance (Chunk 2), section 7.2

rules.exact_text: Where practicable, the name and strength of the product should appear over each blister pocket or be oriented centrally across the pack and remain available to the user up to the point at which the last dose is removed from the blister pack.
rules.reference: MHRA guidance (Chunk 2), section 6.2

rules.exact_text: Where practicable, the name and strength of the product should appear over each blister pocket or be oriented centrally across the pack and remain available to the user up to the point at which the last dose is removed from the blister pack.
rules.reference: MHRA guidance (Chunk 2), section 6.2

rules.exact_text: It will be acceptable to apply the batch number and expiry date to the end of the blister strip; if technically possible this could be applied to both ends of each strip.
rules.reference: MHRA guidance (Chunk 2), section 6.2

rules.exact_text: It will be acceptable to apply the batch number and expiry date to the end of the blister strip; if technically possible this could be applied to both ends of each strip.
rules.reference: MHRA guidance (Chunk 2), section 6.2

rules.exact_text: Calendar packs must be supplied in multiples of 7 and all blister pockets must be labelled with the days of the week.
rules.reference: MHRA guidance (Chunk 2), section 6.5
Human Medicines Regulations 2012, Regulation 261
